q1.prompt: A 32-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis is treated with a long-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor. While her muscle strength improves, she develops significant bradycardia. What is the most appropriate physiological explanation for this side effect?

options.label: Reduced breakdown of acetylcholine released by the vagus nerve.
Enhanced release of norepinephrine from sympathetic terminals.
Blockade of nicotinic receptors on the sinoatrial node.
Decreased levels of acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft.
Direct activation of beta-1 adrenergic receptors by the drug.

q2.prompt: In the synthesis of catecholamines, the enzyme phenylethanolamine-N-methyltransferase (PNMT) is essential for the production of epinephrine. If a patient has a tumor of the adrenal medulla (pheochromocytoma) that is located such that it does not receive venous drainage from the adrenal cortex, which of the following results would be expected?

options.label: Increased conversion of norepinephrine to epinephrine.
High levels of normetanephrine in the urine but low levels of metanephrine.
Activation of D_2 receptors in the target tissues.
Preferential secretion of norepinephrine over epinephrine.
Decreased levels of tyrosine hydroxylase.

q3.prompt: A postsynaptic neuron receives two simultaneous excitatory inputs (Synapse 1 and Synapse 2) and one inhibitory input (Synapse 3). If Synapse 1 produces 10 mV of depolarization, Synapse 2 produces 10 mV of depolarization, and Synapse 3 produces 15 mV of hyperpolarization, which of the following is the most likely outcome if the cell's threshold is 12 mV above its resting potential?

options.label: The cell will hyperpolarize because the inhibitory input is the strongest individual signal.
An action potential will fire because of temporal summation.
No action potential will fire because the net depolarization is only 5 mV.
An action potential will fire because the net change is 20 mV.
The cell will reach threshold because of the "all-or-none" law.

q4.prompt: During an experiment, the extracellular potassium concentration surrounding a neuron is significantly increased. How would this change affect the magnitude of an excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP) generated by the opening of non-selective cation channels?

options.label: The cell will reach a more negative peak potential during the EPSP.
The EPSP magnitude will increase because the K^+ equilibrium potential becomes more negative.
The EPSP magnitude will remain unchanged as it depends only on Na^+ influx.
The EPSP will be replaced by an inhibitory postsynaptic potential (IPSP).
The EPSP magnitude will decrease because the K^+ driving force is reduced.

q5.prompt: A patient is exposed to botulinus toxin. Which of the following sets of findings at the neuromuscular junction would be most consistent with this condition?

options.label: Normal EPP amplitude; normal MEPP amplitude; blocked Ca^(2+) channels.
Decreased EPP amplitude; normal MEPP amplitude; decreased ACh release.
Normal EPP amplitude; decreased MEPP amplitude; increased ACh release.
Increased EPP amplitude; normal MEPP amplitude; blocked nicotinic receptors.
Decreased EPP amplitude; decreased MEPP amplitude; normal ACh release.

q6.prompt: A researcher is studying a novel drug that acts as a potent D_2 receptor antagonist. In which of the following clinical scenarios would this drug most likely be utilized, and what is its effect on the underlying neurochemistry?

options.label: Schizophrenia; to block overactive dopaminergic signaling in the midbrain.
Huntington's disease; to restore GABAergic inhibition in the striatum.
Parkinson's disease; to increase dopaminergic excitation of the direct pathway.
Alzheimer's disease; to increase the synthesis of amyloid plaques.
Myasthenia gravis; to prevent the degradation of acetylcholine.

q7.prompt: A patient with Huntington's disease exhibits characteristic choreiform movements. Which of the following neurochemical changes in the basal ganglia is primarily responsible for these uncontrollable movements?

options.label: Loss of glutamate receptors in the subthalamic nucleus.
Increased sensitivity of D_1 receptors in the motor cortex.
Destruction of GABAergic and cholinergic neurons in the striatum.
Overproduction of acetylcholine in the striatum.
Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra.

q8.prompt: In the process of long-term potentiation (LTP), the NMDA receptor plays a pivotal role. Which of the following conditions is absolutely required for the NMDA-associated channel to permit the influx of calcium?

options.label: A resting membrane potential of -90 mV to keep the channel open.
The presence of glycine to act as a competitive antagonist.
Inactivation of the Na^+/K^+ ATPase to reverse the Na^+ gradient.
Sufficient depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane to displace Mg^(2+) ions.
Binding of GABA to a neighboring GABA_B receptor.

q9.prompt: During the cephalic phase of salivation, which of the following occurs at the neuroeffector junction of the salivary glands to produce a large volume of enzyme-rich saliva?

options.label: Sympathetic release of norepinephrine onto alpha receptors to cause vasodilation.
Somatic activation of myoepithelial cells via nicotinic receptors.
Adrenergic stimulation of cAMP to decrease chloride absorption in the ducts.
Blockade of muscarinic receptors by local paracrine factors.
Parasympathetic release of ACh and VIP to increase secretion and blood flow.

q10.prompt: A 56-year-old man presents with leg weakness that improves with exercise. Electrical recordings from a single skeletal muscle fiber reveal normal miniature end plate potentials (MEPPs). However, low-frequency electrical stimulation of the motor neuron elicits an abnormally small depolarization (EPP) of the muscle fibers. Which mechanism best explains this presentation?

options.label: Blockade of choline reuptake by the presynaptic terminal.
Impaired influx of calcium into the presynaptic terminal due to antibodies.
Excessive activity of acetylcholinesterase in the synaptic cleft.
Autoimmune destruction of postsynaptic nicotinic receptors.
Mutation in the skeletal muscle ryanodine receptor.

q11.prompt: Nitric oxide (NO) is a unique neurotransmitter because it is not stored in vesicles. Which of the following statements best describes its mechanism of action on a target cell?

options.label: It is released by exocytosis in response to a calcium wave.
It opens ligand-gated Na^+ channels to produce rapid depolarization.
It binds to a seven-pass transmembrane G-protein coupled receptor.
It enters the cell via facilitated diffusion and inhibits adenylyl cyclase.
It diffuses into the cell and activates cytosolic guanylyl cyclase to produce cGMP.

q12.prompt: A sensory neuron is stimulated by a persistent pressure. The neuron fires a burst of action potentials at the onset and offset of the stimulus but is silent during the middle of the stimulus period. This behavior is indicative of which of the following?

options.label: A receptor with a small receptive field using spatial summation.
Lateral inhibition that sharpens the contrast of the pressure area.
A tonic receptor encoding the intensity of the steady pressure.
A phasic receptor that encodes the velocity of the stimulus.
A decrease in membrane capacitance that shortens the time constant.

q13.prompt: D-tubocurarine (curare) is administered to a patient to produce muscle relaxation. How does this drug change the electrical properties of the motor end plate?

options.label: It hyperpolarizes the end plate by opening Cl^- channels.
It reduces the amplitude of the EPP by competing for ACh binding sites.
It increases the threshold for firing an action potential in the end plate.
It inhibits acetylcholinesterase, leading to a persistent depolarization block.
It prevents the release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

q14.prompt: A 24-hour urine collection from a patient with suspected pheochromocytoma shows significantly elevated levels of vanillylmandelic acid (VMA). Which of the following biochemical sequences is responsible for the production of this metabolite?

options.label: Sequential action of monoamine oxidase (MAO) and catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT).
Synthesis of arginine into nitric oxide and citrulline.
Reuptake of serotonin followed by aldehyde dehydrogenase activity.
Methylation of dopamine by phenylethanolamine-N-methyltransferase.
Cleavage of neuropeptide Y by extracellular peptidases.

q15.prompt: Strychnine is a potent poison that causes generalized muscle convulsions by interfering with inhibitory neurotransmission. Which of the following is the specific mechanism of strychnine's action?

options.label: It inhibits the Na^+/K^+ ATPase, leading to persistent depolarization.
It stimulates the overproduction of glutamate in the motor cortex.
It blocks the release of GABA from Renshaw cells.
It acts as a competitive antagonist at glycine receptors in the spinal cord.
It prevents the opening of Cl^- channels in the presynaptic terminal.
